title: Download and Use the Latest Release
text:
Download and Use the Latest Release

This guide shows how to download the latest AALI Agent release, configure it, and launch it on your OS.



Download the Latest Release

The latest release includes:

Source code

Executable for Windows (aali-agent-<VERSION>.exe)

Executable for Linux (aali-agent-<VERSION>)

Download from the
Latest AALI Agent Release.

You can also browse all versions on the
AALI Agent Releases Page.

Set Up the Configuration File

The AALI Agent requires a valid config.yaml file. You can provide it in one of two ways:

Place it in the same directory as the executable.

Set the environment variable AALI_CONFIG_PATH to its location.

If no config is found, the agent uses the
default config/config.yaml.

Start the AALI Agent

Run the executable depending on your OS:

Linux



Windows



If successful, the terminal output should look like this:

title: Workflow Design
text:
Workflow Design

Here is how to design and define a new workflow for the AALI Agent using the Agent Configurator.



Workflow Structure

A workflow consists of nodes, connected in a logical order. Each node has:

A name

A type (e.g., exec, chat_interface, fallback)

Optional inputs and outputs

The agent walks through this structure based on instruction flow.

Node Types

Core node types include:

chat_interface: handles front-end interaction

exec: invokes execution environment

llm_call: sends request to LLM Handler

condition: branches the flow

fallback: defines a path if the prior node fails

Exposing Variables

You can expose variables at the workflow level (e.g., collection, validateCode, keywords).
Variables are injected during context setup and can be modified at runtime by the client.

Fallback Logic

Any node can define a fallback_node to redirect execution if it fails.
This makes workflows more robust by providing recovery paths and alternative logic trees.



To build a workflow, use the Agent Configurator and export your definition into the Config service.
You can also validate JSON format manually, or preview node graphs in the Web UI.

title: Workflow Types
text:
Workflow Types

The AALI Agent supports both pre-defined (built-in) workflows and custom workflows defined by users.
Each workflow represents a series of steps executed in sequence to fulfill a task.



Provided Workflows

The AALI Agent includes three built-in workflows, each tailored for a different purpose:

Standard Workflow
Generates Python code snippets based on user input. You specify a collection (e.g. PyMAPDL)
and can optionally set keywords or enable validateCode. The agent returns executable code.

General Workflow
Generates general-purpose text responses not tied to any specific PyAnsys library.
It operates similarly to the standard workflow, but focuses on conversational outputs.

Data Extraction Workflow
Extracts data from sources like local storage or GitHub, storing it in a target database.
Before use, you must configure this workflow using the
AALI Agent Configurator
to provide authentication tokens and extraction parameters.



Custom Workflows

Custom workflows allow you to define your own execution logic. Using the
AALI Agent Configurator, you can:

Define the ordered list of steps the workflow should execute.

Configure workflow nodes and their corresponding variables.

Set default values and logic for runtime overrides.

As described in basic_usage, you can modify any exposed workflow variable
at startup or during execution to adapt the workflow’s behavior dynamically.

title: Troubleshooting
text:
Troubleshooting

Here are common issues encountered when running or integrating the AALI Agent, along with suggested resolutions and debugging tips.



Agent fails to start

Check that the config.yaml file exists and is correctly formatted.

Ensure required backend endpoints (e.g. LLM Handler, Exec, KVDB) are reachable and healthy.

Try running go build manually and inspect for any build-time errors.

WebSocket session hangs or disconnects

Ensure the client sends a valid context message before any instruction.

Confirm the agent logs show connection activity and payload handling.

Check for broken or reused instruction_id values in multi-message streams.

Workflow does not progress

Verify that each workflow node has a valid type, name, and expected inputs.

Ensure that fallback logic is defined in case of failure.

Enable debug mode to trace routing between agent and execution backends.

LLM or Exec returns empty or null

Check that the input payloads are complete and contextually valid.

Review backend logs (LLM Handler, Exec) for errors or validation issues.

Test individual steps with external tools like wscat to isolate the service.

title: Agent Architecture
text:
Agent Architecture

The AALI Agent is the central orchestrator of the entire AALI system.
Its job is to route, validate, and coordinate execution across several connected services.



Execution Backends

The agent interfaces with multiple services to execute instructions and workflows:

FlowKit: Executes Python-based logic, routes instructions, returns state.

Exec: WebSocket-based process controller for isolated task execution.

KVDB: Key-value in-memory database used for runtime session memory and workflow state.

Configuration + Definition Sources

These sources help the agent define what a workflow is, where it runs, and what parameters it expects:

Config Service: Loads workflow definitions, environment defaults, and agent runtime settings.

Agent Variables: Preloaded or injected at runtime, allowing workflows to branch, validate, or adapt.

Communication & Integration

The agent exposes a WebSocket layer and communicates through structured, versioned JSON instructions:

Chat Layer: Any frontend (like AALI Chat) connects via WebSocket to issue instructions and get replies.

Context Management: The agent parses context payloads and uses them to initialize or modify workflows.

Instruction Routing: Messages are routed internally to the correct service via instruction_id.



The architecture is modular, asynchronous, and designed to handle fallback logic, streaming outputs,
and variable resolution across components. It makes the AALI Agent a powerful core in distributed LLM orchestration.

title: Overview
text:
Overview

The AALI system provides an LLM-based application
designed to deliver tailored responses to user queries involving PyAnsys libraries.
It is composed of multiple components, each handling a specific part of the process.

Interacting with these components individually requires boilerplate logic
and internal API knowledge — which is where the AALI Agent steps in.



The Purpose of the AALI Agent

The AALI Agent abstracts complexity across the system and provides a unified API for managing workflows.

It is responsible for:

Communicating with the AALI Chat or other chat interfaces

Handling LLM requests via AALI LLM Handler (vector embedding, keyword extraction, completions)

Communicating with AALI DB for similarity searches

Connecting to AALI Exec for remote code execution

Performing code validation when needed



Customization and Extensibility

The AALI Agent is built around customizable workflows, each defining
a sequence of operations to fulfill a task. Examples include:

Standard workflow:
1. Receive query
2. Embed vectors
3. Extract keywords
4. Perform similarity search
5. Generate final query
6. Call chat completion
7. (Optional) Validate generated code

Custom workflow:
Developers can define their own logic and execution flow when running the agent in development mode.

For more details, see workflow_types.

title: Basic Usage
text:
Basic Usage

The AALI Agent handles communication across the AALI system components, supporting various workflow types
(workflow_types) and enabling interaction through websocket connections or the AALI Chat interface.



Connect to the AALI Agent

After choosing a workflow, you can connect to the AALI Agent by creating a websocket connection.

Example using wscat:

👉 Recommended: Use AALI Chat, which manages connection setup and message flow automatically.

You can also use the AALI Python client library to communicate with the agent programmatically.



Connect to a Workflow

After connection, send a context message:

Supported context properties:

session_type: “workflow” (default) or “exec”

workflow_id: ID of the workflow to execute

exec_id, api_key: Required for exec sessions

jwt_token: Required if authentication is enabled

variables: Optional dictionary of initial workflow inputs

If valid, the agent responds:



Interact Within the Workflow

Once the workflow runs and reaches a chat_interface node, you receive a response.
If no such node exists, the workflow completes silently after context.

title: Execution Control
text:
Execution Control

The AALI Agent manages execution flow using an internal queue and routing logic.
It ensures that instructions are processed in order, can fail gracefully, and support dynamic behavior.



Instruction Routing & Matching

The agent uses instruction_id to match incoming responses with active workflow states.
It routes instructions to the appropriate handler (e.g. Exec, FlowKit) and expects confirmation
responses to maintain reliable flow control.

Fallback Handling

If a step fails or is not fulfilled by the expected backend, fallback paths defined in the
workflow definition are triggered. These allow alternative logic to execute, such as retries,
secondary calls, or error messages.

Variable Injection & Runtime Adaptation

Workflows can expose variables to be injected at runtime via context payloads.
These variables influence branching logic, enable runtime overrides, and support
dynamic workflow shaping based on user input or external state.

Streaming vs Non-Streaming Output

The agent supports both single-message and streamed responses.
In streamed mode, intermediate messages are sent with position and is_last flags
to control incremental updates — essential for live UIs and long operations.



This orchestration layer allows workflows to function across infrastructure boundaries with full control,
adaptability, and fallback logic baked into the execution model.

title: Install the AALI Agent from Scratch
text:
Install the AALI Agent from Scratch

This guide walks you through setting up the AALI Agent from source.
You’ll install required tools, clone the repository, configure your environment, and run the agent.



Install Git and Go

Before you can run the AALI Agent, you must install Git
and the Go programming language. Follow the steps for your OS:

Windows

Install Git for Windows

Install Go via the official Windows guide

Linux



Mac



Clone the Repository

Once Git and Go are installed, clone the repository:

If the repository is private, make sure you have the proper access permissions.

Set Up the Configuration File

You need a valid config.yaml file to run the agent.
An example is available in the
config/config.yaml file:

With everything configured, start the service using:
